summary: Gruber responds to Facebook taking out full-page newspaper ads attacking Apple's iOS 14 privacy changes, which require apps to ask users for permission before tracking them. He argues that Facebook's real goal is to lobby politicians into forcing Apple to roll back privacy protections, since Apple won't voluntarily change course. Gruber emphasizes that Apple isn't blocking ad personalization — it's simply giving users the choice to opt in or out. He compares Facebook's sense of entitlement to user data to telemarketers objecting to do-not-call lists. He also takes a parting shot at the ad's use of a dumb quote mark instead of a proper apostrophe.
key_points: Facebook's full-page newspaper ads are transparently targeting politicians who could force Apple's hand through antitrust regulation, not the general public
Apple's iOS 14 changes don't block ad personalization — they simply give users awareness and control over tracking, making it opt-in rather than invisible
Facebook frames user privacy controls as an attack on small businesses, but their real objection is losing unfettered access to user data they feel entitled to
Facebook's only path to reversing Apple's privacy changes is government intervention, since Apple won't voluntarily back down
The existence of a multi-billion-dollar surveillance advertising industry doesn't create a right to continue harvesting user data without consent
money_quotes: And people say Facebook has hurt the newspaper advertising industry. They're helping!
But in today's world, it's kind of transparent whom Facebook is targeting here: old white politicians.
They want mobile app privacy to go back to the Wild West days of a decade ago, when apps could get away with whatever was technically possible, with all data-hoovering invisible to users.
Apple isn't blocking the ability for Facebook to personalize ads, in any way. Apple is just providing users with control over their own privacy.

Facebook sees Apple providing users with awareness of and control over their online privacy as Apple taking away from Facebook access to something that they believe they rightfully should have free and unfettered access to.
This is no different than telemarketers feeling like you're doing them wrong when you add your phone number to a do-not-call list.

Just because there is now a multi-billion-dollar industry based on the abject betrayal of our privacy doesn't mean the sociopaths who built it have any right whatsoever to continue getting away with it.
Also worth pointing out: the apostrophe in the Facebook newspaper ad's headline is wrong. A big old dumb quote mark.
key_insight: Facebook's campaign against Apple's privacy changes reveals their core belief that user data is theirs by right, when in reality Apple is simply giving users the choice they should have always had.
